title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 11 (Fragment 1)
text:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 11

After a while, someone came and picked Tonino up. That was unpleasant. His legs and arms dragged and dangled in all directions, and he could not do anything about it. He was plunged somewhere much darker. Then he was left to lie amid a great deal of bumping and scraping, as if he were in a box which was being pushed across a floor. When it stopped, he found he could move. He sat up, trembling all over.

He was in the same room as before, but it seemed to be much smaller. He could tell that, if he stood up, his head would brush the little lighted chandelier in the ceiling. So he was larger now; where he had been three inches tall before, he must now be more like nine. The puppets must be too big for their scenery, and the false villa meant to look as if it was some distance away. And, with the Duchess suddenly taken ill, none of her helpers had bothered what size Tonino was. They had simply made sure he was shut up again.

“Tonino,” whispered Angelica.

Tonino whirled around. Half the room was full of a pile of lax puppet bodies. He scanned the cardboard head of the policeman, then his enemy the Hangman, and the white sausage of the baby, and came upon Angelica’s face halfway up the pile. It was her own face, though swollen and tearstained. Tonino clapped his hand to his nose. To his relief, the red beak was gone, though he still seemed to be wearing Mr. Punch’s scarlet nightgown.

“I’m sorry,” he said. His teeth seemed to be chattering. “I tried not to hurt you. Are your bones broken?”

“No—o,” said Angelica. She did not sound too sure. “Tonino, what happened?”

“I hanged the Duchess,” Tonino said, and he felt some vicious triumph as he said it. “I didn’t kill her though,” he added regretfully.

Angelica laughed. She laughed until the heap of puppets was shaking and sliding about. But Tonino could not find it funny. He burst into tears, even though he was crying in front of a Petrocchi.

“Oh dear,” said Angelica. “Tonino, stop it! Tonino—please!” She struggled out from among the puppets and hobbled looming through the room. Her head banged the chandelier and sent it tinkling and casting mad shadows over them as she knelt down beside Tonino. “Tonino, please stop. She’ll be furious as soon as she feels better.” Angelica was wearing Judy’s blue cap and Judy’s blue dress still. She took off the blue cap and held it out to Tonino. “Here. Blow on that. I used the baby’s dress. It made me feel better.” She tried to smile at him, but the smile went hopelessly crooked in her swollen face. Angelica’s large forehead must have hit the floor first. It was now enlarged by a huge red bump. Under it, the grin looked grotesque.

Tonino understood it was meant for a smile and smiled back, as well as he could for his chattering teeth.

“Here.” Angelica loomed back through the room to the pile of puppets and heaved at the Hangman. She returned with his black felt cape. “Put this on.”

Tonino wrapped himself in the cape and blew his nose on the blue cap and felt better.

Angelica heaved more puppets about. “I’m going to wear the policeman’s jacket,” she said. “Tonino—have you thought?”

“Not really,” said Tonino. “I sort of know.”

He had known from the moment he looked at the Duchess. She was the enchanter who was sapping the strength of Caprona and spoiling the spells of the Casa Montana. Tonino was not sure about the Duke—probably he was too stupid to count. But in spite of the Duchess’s enchantments, the spells of the Casa Montana—and the Casa Petrocchi—must still be strong enough to be a nuisance to her. So he and Angelica had been kidnapped to blackmail both houses into stopping making spells. And if they stopped, Caprona would be defeated. The frightening part was that Tonino and Angelica were the only two people who knew, and the Duchess did not care that they knew. It was not only that even someone as clever as Paolo would never think of looking in the Palace, inside a Punch and Judy show: it must mean that the two of them would be dead before anyone found them.

vocabulary.definition: The act of demanding money or another benefit from someone in return for not revealing compromising or damaging information about them.
vocabulary.example: So he and Angelica had been kidnapped to blackmail both houses into stopping making spells.

vocabulary.definition: To start crying suddenly and intensely.
vocabulary.example: He burst into tears, even though he was crying in front of a Petrocchi.

vocabulary.definition: A decorative hanging light fixture with branches for several light bulbs or candles.
vocabulary.example: He could tell that, if he stood up, his head would brush the little lighted chandelier in the ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: Making rapid, repeated sounds; often used to describe teeth shivering from cold or fear.
vocabulary.example: His teeth seemed to be chattering.

vocabulary.definition: To hang or swing loosely.
vocabulary.example: His legs and arms dragged and dangled in all directions, and he could not do anything about it.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or sorcery; someone who enchants.
vocabulary.example: She was the enchanter who was sapping the strength of Caprona and spoiling the spells of the Casa Montana.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell or charm.
vocabulary.example: She was the enchanter who was sapping the strength of Caprona and spoiling the spells of the Casa Montana.

vocabulary.definition: Made bigger in size or extent.
vocabulary.example: It was now enlarged by a huge red bump.

vocabulary.definition: Comically or repulsively ugly or distorted.
vocabulary.example: Under it, the grin looked grotesque.

vocabulary.definition: To lift or haul (a heavy thing) with great effort.
vocabulary.example: ” Angelica loomed back through the room to the pile of puppets and heaved at the Hangman.

vocabulary.definition: To take someone away illegally by force.
vocabulary.example: So he and Angelica had been kidnapped to blackmail both houses into stopping making spells.

vocabulary.definition: To appear as a large or indistinct form, often threateningly.
vocabulary.example: ” She struggled out from among the puppets and hobbled looming through the room.

vocabulary.word: loose collocation: "to take off the cap"
vocabulary.definition: To remove a hat or cap from the head.
vocabulary.example:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 11

After a while, someone came and picked Tonino up.

vocabulary.definition: To have on a loose garment typically worn at night.
vocabulary.example:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 11

After a while, someone came and picked Tonino up.

vocabulary.definition: To jump or dive into something suddenly and deeply.
vocabulary.example: He was plunged somewhere much darker.

vocabulary.definition: A figure of a person or animal controlled by strings or by hand for entertainment.
vocabulary.example: The puppets must be too big for their scenery, and the false villa meant to look as if it was some distance away.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that shows sadness or disappointment about something that has happened.
vocabulary.example: “I didn’t kill her though,” he added regretfully.

vocabulary.definition: To look at all parts carefully in order to detect some feature.
vocabulary.example: He scanned the cardboard head of the policeman, then his enemy the Hangman, and the white sausage of the baby, and came upon Angelica’s face halfway up the pile.

vocabulary.definition: The natural features of a landscape considered in terms of their appearance, especially when picturesque.
vocabulary.example: The puppets must be too big for their scenery, and the false villa meant to look as if it was some distance away.

vocabulary.definition: The act or sound of something being rubbed or scratched harshly.
vocabulary.example: Then he was left to lie amid a great deal of bumping and scraping, as if he were in a box which was being pushed across a floor.

vocabulary.definition: Enlarged by fluid or inflammation.
vocabulary.example: It was her own face, though swollen and tearstained.

vocabulary.definition: Making a light, clear, bell-like sound.
vocabulary.example: Her head banged the chandelier and sent it tinkling and casting mad shadows over them as she knelt down beside Tonino.

vocabulary.definition: Shaking involuntarily, typically as a result of anxiety, excitement, or cold.
vocabulary.example: He sat up, trembling all over.

vocabulary.definition: A great victory or achievement; success.
vocabulary.example:
”

“I hanged the Duchess,” Tonino said, and he felt some vicious triumph as he said it.

vocabulary.definition: Deliberately cruel or violent.
vocabulary.example:
”

“I hanged the Duchess,” Tonino said, and he felt some vicious triumph as he said it.
